{{日本の氏族
|家名 = Kiso no Kuni no Miyatsuko
|家紋 = <!-- 家紋の画像 -->
|家紋名称 =
|本姓 = Unknown
|家祖 = Unknown
|種別 = Unknown
|出身地 = Unknown
|根拠地 = Unknown
|人物 =
|支流 =
}}
'''Kiso no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''kiso no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''kiso kokuzō'') is a fictitious {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} whose name appears only in the {{ill|forged book|ja|偽書}} ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}''.

No records of the Kiso no Kuni no Miyatsuko exist other than the Taiseikyō and works influenced by it<ref name="名前なし-20231105142214">Maeda Ryōtarō, "Reconsideration of the Taihōke-bon 'Shin-shi Keizu' and 'Aso-ke Ryaku Keifu'—The Reconstructed Suwa Traditions," ''Kokugo Kokubun Kenkyū'' No. 161 (Hokkaido University Kokubungakkai, August 2023)</ref>.

== Overview ==
In the Kokuzō Hongi of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}'', the entry '''Kiso no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' appears, and no Kiso no Kuni no Miyatsuko is seen in any sources other than works influenced by the Taiseikyō<ref name="名前なし-20231105142214"/>.

== Forged Genealogy: "Ihon Aso Keizu" ==
In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 31 ([[1956]]), {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓}} visited the Aso family of Miyaji seeking the Aso clan genealogy. What the Aso family provided was called the "Ihon Aso Keizu" marked "donated by Nakada Norinobu." Tanaka considered it established in the mid-Edo period or later, but in reality it was created by {{ill|Nakada Norinobu|ja|中田憲信}} in the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} period. The content of this genealogy was: "Starting with Takeiotake-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|健磐龍命}}) as the ancestor, on the one hand it connects from the {{ill|Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|科野国造}} to the Ōhōri family of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, and on the other hand it connects from the Aso no Kuni no Miyatsuko {{ill|Hayamikatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速瓶玉命}} as ancestor to the Aso Daigūji family; in the midst of this were included all the answers to such questions as the shift from 'hyōtoku' to 'gun-gi Dairyō' (the shift of administrative divisions from {{ill|hyō|ja|評}} to {{ill|gun|ja|郡}}), the granting of the surname 'Uji,' the appointment to 'Aso Gūji,' as well as the position of the Aso clan within the ancient ritsuryō system, the origin of the Aso Gūji, and why in medieval times the Daigūji family came to call themselves 'Uji.'"

Originally, the Aso clan genealogy was included in the ''{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}''. This was a compilation of the "Aso Keizu" which recorded transmissions passed down to the time of Tomosada's son {{ill|Aso Tomotaka|ja|阿蘇友隆}}, covering from the legendary ancestor Korehito in the Kamakura period to {{ill|Aso Koremitsu|ja|阿蘇惟光}} and {{ill|Aso Koreyoshi|ja|阿蘇惟善}}, and further the transmissions up to the time of Koreyoshi's son {{ill|Aso Tomosada|ja|阿蘇友貞}}, to which was added the divine genealogy from {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} to Korehito, and copied by {{ill|Maruyama Kazumi|ja|丸山可澄}} in the {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} era.

However, until the establishment of the Aso clan genealogy included in the ''Zoku Gunsho Ruijū'', variant genealogies were not particularly known. If such a detailed variant genealogy had existed, it would certainly have been adopted when the Aso clan genealogy was created, and no description suggestive of a variant genealogy is seen among the medieval Aso documents. Among these variant genealogies, the notations "donated by Nakada Norinobu" and "compiled by Nakada Norinobu" are notable, which indicates that the currently existing variant genealogy was provided from outside.

In Shōwa 58 ([[1983]]), {{ill|Iida Mizuho|ja|飯田瑞穂}} stated, "I have noticed several examples showing the transition of titles from kuni no miyatsuko → guntoku → dairyō in old places," and, "There have been genealogy-creation specialists in every age, and it is not too far off the mark to think that there is such a background. Among kokugakusha, there are those like {{ill|Suzuki Mayoshi|ja|鈴木真年}} who have sold their name in that world. The transition kuni no miyatsuko → guntoku → dairyō may well have been a reflection of the knowledge and understanding of those people" — there is a view that he implicitly pointed out that the variant genealogy is a forged genealogy.

Also, in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 8 ([[1996]]), {{ill|Murasaki Machiko|ja|村崎真智子}} denied the credibility of the Ihon Aso Keizu. Through classification and lineage analysis of the variant Aso genealogies, Murasaki clarified that the origin of the variant Aso genealogies begins with Nakada Norinobu's version in the early Meiji, and also introduced discussions relating the Ihon Aso Keizu with the Suwa "Shin-shi Keizu," which share some similarities in part. This so-called Shin-shi Keizu was discovered in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 17 ([[1884]]) at the former Ōhōri family of Suwa Taisha's Upper Shrine; from the supplement to the ''Shūho Suwa-shi Keizu'', {{ill|Takei Yukishige|ja|武居幸重}}'s "Aso-shi Keizu Ikken," {{ill|Miyaji Naokazu|ja|宮地直一}}'s ''Suwa-shi'', and others, Murasaki concluded that she had no choice but to conclude that {{ill|Iida Taketō|ja|飯田武郷}}, the Suwa Taisha gūji, made the draft and Nakada Norinobu compiled it as a genealogy. That is, in the genealogies of the Aso no Kuni no Miyatsuko family and the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko family within the Ihon Aso Keizu, there are later forgeries, and Iida and Nakada were involved in these.

Considering the ''{{ill|Wagaku Sōran|ja|和学総覧}}'', Iida and Nakada, and Nakada and {{ill|Suzuki Mayoshi|ja|鈴木真年}}, were disciples of {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, a {{ill|kokugakusha|ja|国学者}}. For these kokugakusha, the history and relationship of gun and hyō were widely-known knowledge from works such as the ''{{ill|Shinpen Hitachi-no-Kuni-shi|ja|新編常陸国誌}}''. Iida, Nakada, and Suzuki were wary of the appearance of those who would doubt the forged genealogies. Therefore, by annotating the forged genealogies with "examples showing the transition of titles from kuni no miyatsuko → guntoku → dairyō," they thought the credibility of the genealogies would be enhanced. In fact, Tanaka, who discovered the Ihon Aso Keizu, carefully examined the genealogy, and, while knowing that late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} kokugakusha had knowledge of "{{ill|hyō|ja|評}}," optimistically judged the content of the variant genealogy saying, "While it is not impossible to forge it entirely, there is no need to doubt that far."

{{ill|Asohin Yasuo|ja|阿蘇品保夫}}, who served as director of the Yatsushiro Municipal Museum and chairman of the Kumamoto Prefecture Cultural Property Protection Council, emphasized "the magnitude of the historical evaluation" of the Aso clan's existence that Nakada utilized, said that only those from {{ill|Aso Korenobu|ja|阿蘇惟宣}} onward in the Aso clan genealogy are credible, and said, "The nobility of the Aso clan is not guaranteed by a forged genealogy of the level of Nakada and others"<ref>Asohin Yasuo, ''Aso-sha to Daigūji: Chūsei no Aso (Shizen to Bunka Aso Sensho 2)'' (Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1999)</ref>.

{{ill|Maeda Ryōtarō|ja|間枝遼太郎}} argued that the theory in the ''Aso-shi Ryaku Keizu'' that the {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇氏}} and {{ill|Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|科野国造}} are of the same clan, and the temporal consistency of offices such as "hyōtoku," have already been denied by prior historical examination, and on this basis demonstrated that the ''Aso-shi Ryaku Keizu'' is a genealogy that '''does not have the power to clarify ancient historical facts'''<ref name="名前なし-20231105142214"/>.{{main|Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}

== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist}}
{{Notelist}}{{デフォルトソート:きそのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:架空の国家を舞台とした作品]]
[[Category:偽作]]
[[Category:長野県の歴史|偽きそのくにのみやつこ]]
[[Category:信濃国|偽きそのくにのみやつこ]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:木蘇国造]]
